title: How to ventilate your home without losing heat
description: How to ventilate a UK home without wasting heat: purge ventilation, extractor fans, trickle vents and heat recovery, with real costs at 2026 energy rates.
blurb: Short sharp airing beats a window on tilt: the real heat cost of ventilation, with fixes from trickle vents to MVHR.
lede: Every winter the same argument plays out in British homes: one person opens a window because the kitchen has steamed up, and another shuts it because the heating is on. Both have a point. A family of four puts ten to fifteen litres of water into the air of their home every day through cooking, showering, breathing and drying clothes, and if that moisture has no way out it ends up as condensation on the windows and mould behind the wardrobe. Yet heated air costs money, and throwing it out of the window feels like burning cash. The good news is that ventilating well costs far less than most people assume, provided you do it the right way round.
short_answer: Ventilate hard and briefly rather than gently all day. A ten-minute whole-house purge with windows wide open swaps all the stale air for about 8p of gas heat, because the warmth stored in your walls and furniture stays put. A window left on tilt all evening costs around 21p a night, roughly £30 over a heating season, while extractor fans used at the source cost pennies. Figures use the Ofgem price cap for July to September 2026.

sections.h2: Why you cannot simply seal your home shut
sections.html:
Your home produces a remarkable amount of water vapour. Cooking a meal can release up to three litres into the air, a shower adds around half a litre, a load of washing dried on an indoor airer gives off two litres or more, and a sleeping family breathes out another litre or so overnight. Across a typical household that adds up to ten to fifteen litres a day, every single day.

If that moisture cannot escape, it condenses on the coldest surfaces it can find: window frames, the corners of external walls, the cold patch behind the wardrobe. From there you get black mould, musty smells, damaged plaster and a genuine health risk. Damp air is also harder to heat, and damp walls conduct heat out of the building faster than dry ones, so a sealed-up damp house ends up costing more to keep warm, not less.

This is why ventilation and draught-proofing are not opposites. A draught is uncontrolled air leaking through gaps you never chose, all day, whether you need it or not. Ventilation is air you let in deliberately, where and when it does some good. The aim is to close the gaps you cannot control and make proper use of the openings you can.

sections.h2: The golden rule: ventilate hard, briefly, and at the source
sections.html:
The cheapest way to swap stale, damp air for fresh is a purge. Open windows wide on opposite sides of the house, ideally with internal doors open so air flows straight through, for five to ten minutes, then close everything. The air changes completely, but the heat stored in your walls, floors, furniture and radiators barely moves, and it rewarms the fresh air within minutes. German households have done this routinely for generations, and it is the method building scientists recommend for naturally ventilated homes.

The second habit is dealing with moisture where it is made, before it spreads through the house:

  • Keep lids on pans and run the cooker hood while you cook, vented outside if possible.
  • Run the bathroom fan during a shower and for fifteen to twenty minutes afterwards, with the bathroom door closed so the moist air leaves through the wall rather than drifting onto the landing. A typical fan costs well under a penny an hour, as our extractor fan running cost guide shows.
  • Dry clothes outdoors when you can. If you must dry indoors, do it in one room with the door shut and a window cracked, rather than draping wet washing over radiators around the house.
  • Shut kitchen and bathroom doors while the steam clears, so the rest of the house never receives the moisture in the first place.

sections.h2: What ventilation actually costs you in lost heat
sections.html:
Air holds surprisingly little heat. Warming one cubic metre of air by one degree takes about 0.34 watt hours, so even replacing every scrap of air in a medium-sized house throws away only around one kilowatt hour of heat on a typical winter day. The table shows what common ventilation habits cost in reheated air, assuming 20°C indoors, 8°C outdoors and a gas boiler doing the reheating.

Ventilation eventAir replacedHeat lostCost to reheat
Bathroom fan after a shower (15 minutes)20 m³0.08 kWhunder 1p
Cooker hood on medium while cooking (30 minutes)100 m³0.4 kWhabout 3p
Whole-house purge, windows wide open (10 minutes)250 m³1.0 kWhabout 8p
One window on tilt all evening (8 hours)640 m³2.6 kWhabout 21p
Continuous whole-house airflow, no heat recovery (24 hours)3,000 m³12.2 kWhabout £1
The same airflow through MVHR recovering 90 per cent3,000 m³1.2 kWhabout 10p

Heat replaced by a 90 per cent efficient gas boiler at 7.33p per kWh, Ofgem cap July to September 2026. Assumes 20°C indoors, 8°C outdoors and air holding 0.34 Wh per m³ per degree; a 250 m³ house is roughly a three to four bedroom semi.

The pattern is clear. Brief, purposeful ventilation costs pennies. What hurts is duration. The window on tilt all evening costs around 21p a night, which is £30 or so across a five-month heating season for a single window, and closer to £95 if it stays tilted day and night. Continuous background airflow at the rate building regulations aim for would cost nearly £1 a day in lost heat if you achieved it with open windows alone, which is exactly why heat recovery systems exist.

sections.h2: Trickle vents, humidistat fans and other cheap controls
sections.html:
Between a sealed box and an open window sits a set of low-cost controls that let moisture out steadily without dumping your heat.

  • Trickle vents are the small slots at the top of modern window frames. They exist to provide background ventilation and they should normally stay open through winter, especially in bedrooms and wet rooms. The airflow through them is modest, and closing them is one of the most common causes of windows streaming with condensation by morning.
  • Humidistat extractor fans switch themselves on when the air gets damp and off again once it dries, so you get extraction exactly when it is needed and not a minute longer. They are a sensible like-for-like upgrade when a basic bathroom fan fails.
  • Continuous-running dMEV fans trickle along at a few watts around the clock and step up when the room is in use. Many newer homes have them fitted as standard, and they cost only a few pounds a year to run.
  • A morning bedroom purge matters more than people realise. Overnight breathing leaves bedroom air at its dampest just after you wake, so throw the window wide for ten minutes while the kettle boils, then shut it.

One more point on timing: ventilate while the house is warm rather than letting it go cold first. Warm air carries moisture out far more effectively than cold air, and the fabric of a warm house reheats the incoming air quickly. A cold, damp house with the heating off all day and the windows shut tight is the worst combination of all.

sections.h2: Heat recovery: keeping the warmth while the air changes
sections.html:
If you want continuous fresh air with almost none of the heat penalty, the gold standard is mechanical ventilation with heat recovery, usually shortened to MVHR. A whole-house system extracts stale air from kitchens and bathrooms and supplies fresh air to living rooms and bedrooms, passing both airstreams through a heat exchanger that transfers up to 90 per cent of the outgoing warmth into the incoming air. In the table above, that turns nearly £1 a day of ventilation heat loss into about 10p. The catch is that MVHR only makes sense in a home that is already airtight; in a leaky house the air simply bypasses the system, and a full retrofit with ducting to every room runs to several thousand pounds.

Two retrofit-friendly options suit ordinary houses better:

  • Single-room heat recovery fans replace a standard extractor with a unit built around a ceramic heat store. The fan reverses direction every minute or so, warming the core as stale air leaves and releasing that heat into the fresh air coming in. Manufacturers claim 70 to 80 per cent heat recovery, and they suit bathrooms and bedrooms with a stubborn condensation problem.
  • Positive input ventilation takes the opposite approach. A quiet loft-mounted fan pushes filtered air down into the landing, gently pressurising the house so damp air is squeezed out through the natural leakage points. It is one of the most effective whole-house cures for condensation and the fan costs only a few pounds a year to run, though the incoming air does still need heating. Our guide to PIV units covers how they work and what they cost.

And when the air outside is as damp as the air inside, a dehumidifier earns its keep. It pulls water out of the air without exchanging any of it, and every watt it draws ends up as heat in the room. Our dehumidifier running cost guide has the full numbers.

Stale, damp air is the enemy of a warm home, not fresh air. Ventilation done well costs pennies: a whole-house purge is about 8p, a bathroom fan after a shower is under 1p, and a cooker hood through dinner is about 3p. What drains money is ventilation done slowly, above all the window left on tilt for hours while the heating fights it.

So purge briefly with windows wide, extract steam at the source with the door shut, keep trickle vents open in winter, and air bedrooms each morning. Pair that with proper draught-proofing of the gaps you never chose, and you get a drier, healthier house that is cheaper to heat than a sealed damp one. If condensation persists after all of that, a PIV unit or heat recovery fan will usually fix it for less than the cost of redecorating a mouldy bedroom.

faq.q: Is it cheaper to open a window wide for ten minutes or leave it slightly open all day?
faq.a: Wide and brief wins comfortably. A ten-minute whole-house purge costs around 8p in reheated air because the heat stored in walls and furniture stays behind, while a single window on tilt for eight hours costs about 21p and lets the fabric of the room chill down. Over a five-month heating season the tilted window habit costs roughly £30 per window.

faq.q: Do trickle vents waste heat in winter?
faq.a: They lose a small amount, but they should normally stay open. The airflow through a trickle vent is modest and it provides the background ventilation a modern, well-sealed home needs. Closing them is one of the most common causes of heavy condensation on bedroom windows, and the damp that follows costs more than the trickle of warm air ever did.

faq.q: Does running the bathroom extractor fan waste heating money?
faq.a: Barely. A typical fan draws 5 to 30 watts, well under a penny an hour to run, and fifteen minutes of extraction after a shower removes about 8p worth of heat at most, usually under 1p. Skipping it means half a litre of water spreads through the house instead, which leads to condensation and mould that cost far more to put right.

faq.q: Can a dehumidifier replace opening windows?
faq.a: Partly. A dehumidifier removes moisture without exchanging any air, and all the electricity it uses ends up as heat in the room, so it is a good option in milder damp weather or for drying laundry indoors. It does not replace fresh air entirely, though, since you still need some air change to clear carbon dioxide, smells and indoor pollutants.

faq.q: Is whole-house heat recovery (MVHR) worth fitting in an existing home?
faq.a: Usually only if the house is already very airtight, for example after a deep retrofit. MVHR can recover up to 90 per cent of the heat in outgoing air, but in a leaky house the air bypasses the system and the several thousand pound installation never pays back. For ordinary homes, single-room heat recovery fans or a positive input ventilation unit are far cheaper and deal with most condensation problems.
